doc.title: Held-to-maturity debt — purchase and interest
doc.event: On {issueDate}, Juno Co. purchases {face} of {statedRateAnnual}, {termYears} bonds ({paymentsPerYear}) to hold to maturity, when the market rate is {marketRateAnnual}. Prepare the purchase entry and the first interest receipt.

variants.label: Bought below face — revenue runs ABOVE the coupon

entries.caption: Purchase HTM bonds at the market price

lines.label: at cost (PV at market)
lines.why: One account, carried at amortized cost — Kieso nets the discount INTO Debt Investments rather than using a separate contra (the investor's simplification).
lines.trap: Not at face. The investment starts at what you paid and amortizes UP toward face.

lines.label: price paid
lines.why: Cash out at the market price.
lines.trap: HTM classification requires the intent AND ability to hold to maturity — the label is a commitment, not a preference.

entries.caption: Receive the semiannual coupon — effective-interest revenue

lines.label: the fixed coupon
lines.why: Same contractual coupon as Ch. 13 — now arriving instead of leaving.
lines.trap: Cash is still not the revenue. Same trap, other chair.

lines.label: amortization walks cost up
lines.why: The discount amortizes by debiting the investment itself — carrying amount climbs the schedule toward face.
lines.trap: Investor-side discount amortization DEBITS the asset (grows it); flipping it shrinks your investment into fiction.

lines.label: CV × market × 6/12
lines.why: Identical number to the issuer's interest expense — one schedule, two chairs. Revenue = carrying amount × market rate.
lines.trap: Face × coupon gives the CASH, never the revenue. The market rate at purchase runs the revenue forever.

computationPaths.narration: The Ch. 13 schedule, re-read from the buyer's chair: issue price = your cost; interest expense column = your interest revenue; carrying value = your investment balance. One table, both sides of the trade.

variants.label: Bought above face — revenue runs BELOW the coupon

lines.label: CV × market × 6/12
lines.why: You paid extra for an above-market coupon; the premium amortizes AGAINST revenue, so revenue < cash.
lines.trap: Premium at purchase = you overpaid for coupons — part of each coupon is your own money coming back, not income.

lines.label: premium walks cost down
lines.why: The investment amortizes DOWN toward face — credit side this time.
lines.trap: Direction check: discount purchase → investment grows; premium purchase → investment shrinks. Both land exactly at face at maturity.

memorize.body: HTM = amortized cost. Interest revenue = carrying amount × market rate at purchase × time. Same schedule as the issuer — expense column becomes your revenue column.
Two chairs, one table: everything you memorized in Ch. 13 flips sides but keeps its number. Discount still means 'the rate story favored the buyer.'

HTM never marks to market — fair value changes are ignored unless there's a CREDIT LOSS (impairment), which books an allowance against income. And selling HTM early can taint the whole portfolio's classification.

questions.prompt: HTM bonds bought at 461,391 (face 500,000, 8% coupon, 10% market, semiannual). Period-1 interest REVENUE =

distractors.feedback: 20,000 is the coupon CASH. Revenue = carrying amount × market rate: 461,391 × 5% = 23,070 — the discount amortization is also income.
The market rate applies to your COST (the carrying amount), not to face — face isn't what you invested.

build.scaffold: Run the Ch. 13 machine from the other chair: cost in, coupon cash + amortization vs revenue each period. The separate-discount-account is a decoy — the investor amortizes the investment itself.
